1.6: . Hebrew, son of Hebrews ; That is to say, their fathers, who were not Hellenistic or who had not intermarried with the Greeks, had preserved the very language of their ancestors. Cf. Acts of the Apostles,
1.13: The Greek Fathers and most commentators understand here by courtroom , The emperor's palace, which at the time was that of Nero. It is certain that this name was given to the residence of the provincial governors, where the emperor himself stayed during his travels. It could therefore also have been given to the palace where he resided while in Rome.
1.22: From Caesar ; That is to say, of Nero, in whose court the Apostle had made conversions. From Caesar's house. These were Christians in the service of the emperor, but it is unknown who they were.
1.12: I myself was seized by Christ . The Apostle is referring to what happened to him on the road to Damascus. See Acts
1.10: You missed the opportunity, You were kept so busy that you were unable to give me proof of these feelings; that is to say, you were prevented from doing so.
1.15: a salary and expense account , No church, except yours, has given me any of its temporal goods for the spiritual goods it received from me. From Macedonia . See Acts of the Apostles,
1.25: Epaphroditus He was a Philippian whom his compatriots had sent to Rome to bring alms to Saint Paul, who was imprisoned. There he had been very ill. After his recovery, he was entrusted by the Apostle to deliver this letter to Philippi.
1.2: Euodia and Syntyche . They were either two deaconesses or two high-ranking women whom Saint Paul exhorts to harmony. The nature of their divisions is unknown.
1.26: ); Saint Paul thus calls the false apostles, either because of the impudence and the relentlessness with which they tore apart the true apostles of Jesus Christ with their slander, or because after having left Judaism to become Christians, they returned to it, in a way, by wanting to preserve circumcision and the other practices of the law, imitating in this the dogs, who return to what they have vomited, as it is said in Proverbs,
1.17: . There are many , etc., no longer the Judaizing doctors of verse
1.20: Our city is in the heavens ; we already live in heaven in spirit, through our feelings and our hope.
1.3: My faithful companion. Companion and in Greek syzyge , which, according to several sources, should be taken as a proper name. In any case, we don't know who he is. ― With Clement . Origen and Saint Jerome tell us that this Clement is the one who became Pope Saint Clement. It is believed that he was born in Rome around the year
1.30: AD, and that he was the second successor—others say the immediate successor—of Saint Peter on the See of Rome. During his pontificate, he wrote a famous letter to the Corinthians. He suffered martyrdom under the Emperor Trajan.
1.4: Rejoice! , was the common greeting formula among the Greeks.
1.16: In Thessaloniki. See Acts of the Apostles,

2.1: If, therefore, there is any encouragement in Christ, if there is any consolation of love, if there is any fellowship of the spirit, if there is any tenderness and compassion,
2.2: Make my joy complete: be of one mind, one love, one soul, one feeling.
2.3: Do nothing out of rivalry or vainglory, but let each of you, in all humility, regard others as above yourself.
2.4: Each person taking into account, not their own interests, but those of others.
2.5: Have in you the same mindset that Christ Jesus had:
2.6: Although he was in the condition of God, he did not greedily cling to his equality with God.,
2.7: But he emptied himself, taking the form of a slave, being made in human likeness, and being found in appearance as a man,
2.8: He humbled himself, becoming obedient unto death, even death on a cross.
2.9: That is why God exalted him to the highest place and bestowed on him the name that is above every name,
2.10: so that in the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth,
2.11: and that every tongue confess, to the glory of God the Father, that Jesus Christ is Lord.
2.12: Therefore, my beloved, as you have always been obedient, so now, not only as in my presence, but much more in my absence, work out your salvation with fear and trembling.,
2.13: for it is God who works in you to will and to act in order to fulfill his good purpose.
2.14: Act in all things without murmuring or hesitating,
2.15: so that you may be blameless, pure, children of God without fault in the midst of this perverse and corrupt people, in whose midst you shine like stars in the world,
2.16: having received the word of life, I will be able to boast on the day of Christ that I did not run in vain, nor labor in vain.
2.17: And even if my blood were to serve as a libation in the sacrifice and service of your faith, I rejoice and congratulate you.
2.18: You too should rejoice and share in my joy.
2.19: I hope in the Lord Jesus to send Timothy to you soon, so that I myself may feel courageous upon hearing news of you.,
2.20: because I have no one who is so united with me in feelings, to sincerely take to heart what concerns you.
2.21: Indeed, they all have their own interests in mind, not those of Jesus Christ.
2.22: You know that he is of proven virtue, that he has devoted himself to me, like a child to his father, in the service of the Gospel.
2.23: I hope to send it to you as soon as I see a resolution to my situation.
2.24: And I also hope in the Lord that I myself may come soon.
2.25: In the meantime, I thought it necessary to send you Epaphroditus, my brother, my companion in labors and battles, who had come from you to provide for my needs.
2.26: because he wanted to see you all again and he was very distressed that you had learned of his illness.
2.27: He was indeed sick to the point of death, but God had mercy on him, and not on him only, but also on me, so that I would not have sorrow upon sorrow.
2.28: So I made more haste to send it to you, so that joy might return to you when you saw it and that I myself might be less sad.
2.29: Therefore, receive him in the Lord with full joy and honor such men.
2.30: for it was for the work of Christ that he came close to death, having risked his life, in order to make up for you in the service which you could not render me.

3.1: Furthermore, my brothers, rejoice in the Lord. Writing the same things to you costs me nothing, and it is beneficial to you.
3.2: Beware of these dogs, beware of these bad workers, beware of these false circumcised men.
3.3: For we are the true circumcision, we who worship God in the Spirit, who glory in Christ Jesus, and do not trust in the flesh.
3.4: And yet, as for me, I too would have reason to place my trust in the flesh. If someone else believes they can do it, I can do it far more.,
3.5: a circumcised man of the eighth day, of the race of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin; a Hebrew, son of Hebrews; a Pharisee, as regards the Law; ;
3.6: a persecutor of the Church, as far as zeal and righteousness are concerned: blameless.
3.7: But these titles, which were precious advantages to me, I now consider a loss for the sake of Christ.
3.8: Yes, indeed, I still consider them a loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord. For his sake I was willing to lose everything, considering everything rubbish, in order that I might gain Christ.
3.9: and to be found in him, not having my own righteousness, which comes from the law, but that which is born of faith in Christ, the righteousness which comes from God on the basis of faith,
3.10: in order to know him and the power of his resurrection, to be admitted to the communion of his sufferings, becoming conformed to him in his death,
3.11: to achieve, if I can, the resurrection of the dead.
3.12: Not that I have already taken hold of it, or have already arrived at my goal, but I press on to take hold of that for which Christ took hold of me.
3.13: For my part, brothers, I do not consider myself to have grasped it, but I do only one thing: forgetting what is behind me and straining with all my might toward what is ahead,
3.14: I press on to the goal to win the prize to which God has called me from above in Christ Jesus.
3.15: Let this be our feelings, all of us who have reached manhood, and if on any point you have different thoughts, God will enlighten you on that also.
3.16: However, from the point where we have arrived, let us walk as we have already done so far.
3.17: You too, brothers, should imitate me and keep your eyes on those who walk according to the pattern you have in us.
3.18: For there are many who walk as enemies of the cross of Christ. I have often spoken to you about this, and I speak of it again now with tears:
3.19: Their end is destruction, for they make their belly their god and glory in their shame, having no taste for anything but earthly things.
3.20: For us, our citizenship is in heaven, and from there we await a Savior, the Lord Jesus Christ.,
3.21: who will transform our wretched body, making it like his glorious body, by his powerful virtue which subjects all things to him.

4.1: Therefore, my dear and beloved brothers, my joy and crown, stand firm in the Lord, my beloved.
4.2: I exhort Euodia and I urge Syntyche to be in good understanding in the Lord.
4.3: And you too, my faithful companion, I ask you to come to their aid, they who have fought for the Gospel with me, with Clement, and my other collaborators whose names are in the book of life.
4.4: Rejoice in the Lord at all times; I will say it again: Rejoice!.
4.5: Let your moderation be known to all men: the Lord is near.
4.6: Do not be anxious about anything, but in every situation, by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving, present your requests to God.
4.7: And the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus.
4.8: Finally, brothers, whatever is true, whatever is honorable, whatever is just, whatever is pure, whatever is lovely, whatever is highly praiseworthy—if there is any virtue or praise—think about such things.
4.9: What you have learned and received, what you have heard me say and seen me do, practice it and the God of peace will be with you.
4.10: I rejoiced greatly in the Lord that I saw your former feelings towards me bloom again; you certainly had them, but you had missed the opportunity.
4.11: I don't speak this way because of my needs, because I have learned to be self-sufficient with what I have.
4.12: I know how to live in want and I know how to live in plenty. In every situation I have learned the secret of being content and hungry, of living in plenty and of being in need.
4.13: I can do all things through him who strengthens me.
4.14: However, you did well to share in my distress.
4.15: Philippians, you also know that in the early days of my preaching, when I left Macedonia, no church opened an account for my salary and expenses except you alone.
4.16: Because you sent me to Thessaloniki, first once, then a second time, enough to provide for my needs.
4.17: It's not that I'm looking for donations, what I'm looking for is the increasing profit in your account.
4.18: Now I have plenty of everything and I am full, I am satisfied, having received from Epaphroditus the gifts from you, like a fragrant offering, an acceptable sacrifice, pleasing to God.
4.19: And my God will supply all your needs according to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus.
4.20: To our God and Father be glory forever and ever, amen.
4.21: Greet all the saints in Jesus Christ. The brothers who are with me send you greetings.
4.22: All the saints greet you, and especially those of Caesar's house.
4.23: May the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit.
